category_title: Male adolescents at risk of dropping out in vocational training programs
minimal_context: The student is reading a theoretical science text about electrical resistance and writing down definitions in a general classroom setting.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Please read the chapter on the theoretical principles of electrical resistance and write down the definitions.

STUDENT: Is there a practical component to this? It looks like it just involves theory.

TUTOR: Just focus on reading the text and writing the definitions for now.

STUDENT: But this has absolutely no connection to our lives or what we actually do.

TUTOR: Keep trying to get through the reading activity.

STUDENT: (Closes the book) I get bored when it's like this. I've lost interest in the activity and I'm just disaffected with the lesson.

options.A: Use the journalist method by speaking in short sentences, talking briefly and concisely to mention only the main details of the electrical resistance text.
options.B: Construct a scenario to contextualize the module and connect it to the student's life, requiring him to relate the conceptual science to real-life issues.
options.C: Ask the student questions directly about the electrical resistance text, while also allowing him ample time to give his response during the science lesson.
options.D: Touch the student on the shoulder while he is seated at his desk during the theoretical science lesson about the principles of electrical resistance.
